coredata.dc:title: The carbon dots with near-infrared electrochemiluminescence emission and low excitation potential for ultrasensitive analysis of microRNA-222
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yu-Zhuo
Liu, Rui
Zeng, Ying
Liao, Ye-Yu
Liu, Jia-Li
Chai, Ya-Qin
Yuan, Ruo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen and sulfur codoped near-infrared emission carbon dots (N,S-NIR-CDs) were used as neoteric electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitters with high efficiency to construct a biosensor for ultrasensitive analysis of microRNA-222 (miRNA-222), which has been regarded as prospective biomarkers for early cancer diagnosis. Impressively, compared to conventional CDs with short ECL wavelength and large excitation voltage, N,S-NIR-CDs possessed wide sp2 domains and narrow band gap, leading to the near-infrared ECL emission at 680 nm and low excitation potential from −1.0 to 0 V, which not only decreased the photochemical damage for biomolecules but also reduced the side reaction. Moreover, an improved T7 Exonuclease induced cascade amplification (EICA) strategy was designed to transform trace miRNA-222 into plentiful output DNA in dual-cycle for greatly improving the target conversion efficiency. Therefore, by combining the N,S-NIR-CDs as neoteric ECL emitters and EICA strategy as signal amplifier, the constructed ECL biosensor could realize the ultrasensitive analysis of miRNA-222 with a identification limit of 5.65 aM and successfully employed in miRNA-222 analysis from liver cancer cell (HCCLM3 and MHCC-97L) lysate. This strategy developed a kind of neoteric CDs with NIR ECL emission and low excitation potential to establish a highly efficient biosensor for biomarker analysis, which prepared a road for early diseases diagnosis and lied a foundation for biological ECL imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Near-infrared electrochemiluminescence
Biosensor
MicroRNA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: T7 Exo induced cascade amplification strategy.
Construction of ECL biosensor and its measurement procedure.
Possible ECL mechanism of N,S-NIR-CDs/S2O8 2− ECL system.
Comparison of the N,S-NIR-CDs and N,S-B-CDs

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176153, 22374122, 22174113 and 22304141), Chongqing Postdoctoral Innovative Talent Support Program (7820100788) and Chongqing Postdoctoral Natural Science Foundation (CSTB2023NSCQ-BHX0009).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (inset: size distribution), (B) AFM image (inset: height profile), (C) FT-IR spectrum, (D) XPS survey and its element content percentage, (E) XRD and (F) Raman spectra of N,S-NIR-CDs.
(A) UV − vis absorption curve of N,S-NIR-CDs. (B) FL spectra of N,S-NIR-CDs. (C) ECL emission spectrum of N,S-NIR-CDs in S2O8 2−. (D) ECL emission spectrum of S2O8 2−.

(A) ECL-Time curves of (a) N,S-NIR-CDs in 50 mM S2O8 2−, (b) bare GCE in 50 mM S2O8 2− and (c) N,S-NIR-CDs in PBS. (B) DPV curves of N,S-NIR-CDs in (a) PBS and (b) deoxygenated PBS. (C) DPV curves of N,S-NIR-CDs in (a) PBS and (b) S2O8 2−.

(A) Synthesis materials and processes of N,S-NIR-CDs and N,S-B-CDs. (B) Normalized FL and (C) Normalized ECL spectrum N,S-NIR-CDs (red line) and N,S-B-CDs (blue line). (D) DPV curves of N,S-B-CDs in (a) PBS and (b) deoxygenated PBS. (E) Raman spectra of N,S-B-CDs.
PAGE characterization of (A) EICA strategy and (B) construction of ECL biosensor.
(A) Increased ECL intensity for detection of different concentration of miRNA-222 (a to h: 10 aM to 100 pM); (B) Related calibration curve.
(A) Selectivity and (B) Stability of proposed ECL biosensor.
ECL intensity of the proved biosensor of different number of cancer cells lysates (MHCC-97L and HCCLM3).
(A) Synthesis of N,S-NIR-CDs and ECL Emission Spectrum. (B) T7 Exo Induced Cascade Amplification (EICA) Strategy of MiRNA-222. (C) Establishment of The Biosensor and Relative ECL Signal Response.
Comparison of different CDs as ECL emitters.

head.ce:title: The carbon dots with near-infrared electrochemiluminescence emission and low excitation potential for ultrasensitive analysis of microRNA-222

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Oncology, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Oncology, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen and sulfur codoped near-infrared emission carbon dots (N,S-NIR-CDs) possessed wide sp2 domains and narrow band gap, leading to the near-infrared ECL emission at 680 nm and low excitation potential from −1.0 to 0 V, which not only decreased the photochemical damage for biomolecules but also reduced the side reaction. Therefore, N,S-NIR-CDs exhibited high ECL efficiency, which lied a strong foundation for the subsequent establishment of biosensing platform.

In this work, nitrogen and sulfur codoped near-infrared emission carbon dots (N,S-NIR-CDs) were used as neoteric electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitters with high efficiency to construct a biosensor for ultrasensitive analysis of microRNA-222 (miRNA-222), which has been regarded as prospective biomarkers for early cancer diagnosis. Impressively, compared to conventional CDs with short ECL wavelength and large excitation voltage, N,S-NIR-CDs possessed wide sp2 domains and narrow band gap, leading to the near-infrared ECL emission at 680 nm and low excitation potential from −1.0 to 0 V, which not only decreased the photochemical damage for biomolecules but also reduced the side reaction. Moreover, an improved T7 Exonuclease induced cascade amplification (EICA) strategy was designed to transform trace miRNA-222 into plentiful output DNA in dual-cycle for greatly improving the target conversion efficiency. Therefore, by combining the N,S-NIR-CDs as neoteric ECL emitters and EICA strategy as signal amplifier, the constructed ECL biosensor could realize the ultrasensitive analysis of miRNA-222 with a identification limit of 5.65 aM and successfully employed in miRNA-222 analysis from liver cancer cell (HCCLM3 and MHCC-97L) lysate. This strategy developed a kind of neoteric CDs with NIR ECL emission and low excitation potential to establish a highly efficient biosensor for biomarker analysis, which prepared a road for early diseases diagnosis and lied a foundation for biological ECL imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), as an emerging analytical technology, has been diffusely deployed in disease analysis [1], food monitoring [2], environment assessment [3], etc. Recently, growing research has been dedicated in the development of near-infrared (NIR) ECL emission materials due to the intrinsic advantages of less photochemical damage and deeper tissue penetration. However, most NIR emissive nanomaterials (such as MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) [4], Cu Nanoclusters (NCs) [5], Ag NCs [6] and Eu-MOF [7]) are consist of heavy metal elements, which possessed some unavoidable shortcomings like excessive toxicity, expensive cost and poor biocompatibility. Hence, it is of vital importance to develop a kind of nanomaterials with NIR ECL emission wavelength (>650 nm) but without heavy metal elements. Carbon dots (CDs), a class of nanoparticles with diameter less than 10 nm, has the advantages of excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis and low cost [8,9]. Whereas, the maximum ECL emission wavelength of most CDs was 450–550 nm approximately [10,11], which hampered the further development of CDs as ECL emitters owing to the potential photodamage for biotic organization. Herein, a nitrogen and sulfur codoped NIR CDs (N,S-NIR-CDs) were synthesized, which not only possessed a NIR ECL emission at 680 nm to reduce the photochemical damage but also had a low excitation potential range to avoid the side reaction.

Over the years, enzyme induced nucleic acid amplification strategy [12,13] has attracted increasing attention, which could tremendously enhance the biomolecules conversion efficiency. Among many enzymes, exonuclease (Exo) has been wildly devoted in the nucleic acid amplification strategy due to the high efficiency of cleavage. However, traditional nucleic acid amplification strategy induced by Exo usually produced the output DNA in single-cycle [14,15], ignoring the recycling of the output product, which impeded the further improvement of signal amplification efficiency. In this work, T7 Exo induced cascade amplification (EICA) strategy was developed to produce the output DNA in dual-cycle, greatly enhancing the amplification efficiency.

Herein, we developed an ECL biosensor based on N,S-NIR-CDs as novel ECL emitters and EICA strategy as highly efficient signal amplifier to implement the ultrasensitive analysis of miRNA-222, which was closely correlated with the liver cancer. As displayed in Scheme 1 A, N,S-NIR-CDs were synthesized through solvothermal method, which exhibited strong NIR ECL emission at 680 nm. Then, EICA strategy was shown in Scheme 1B. First, H1 could be opened with the help of target miRNA-222 (T) to form the H1-T (P1). Second, H2 were hybridized with P1 to form H2-P1, while the H2-P1 could be cut out with assistance of T7 Exo to release the P1 and P2. On the one hand, P1 could hybridize with other H2 in the Cycle 1. On the other hand, P2 could react with H3 in Cycle 2. Third, the hybridization product H3-P2 could be cut out by T7 Exo to form P3 as output DNA and release P2 for the next cycle. Finally, after many cycles of amplification, small amounts of miRNA-222 could transform to large number of P2 and P3, and both of them could act as output DNA to participate in the reaction on the surface of the electrodes. Furthermore, Scheme 1C illustrated the construction of ECL biosensor. In the beginning, quenching probe (QP) labeled with ferrocene (Fc) were incubated with capture probe (CP) to quench the ECL signals of biosensor. Afterwards, output DNA (P2 and P3) were combined with CP to form CP-P2/CP-P3 and release QP, which could recover the ECL respond and realize the sensitive analysis for miRNA-222 from 10 aM to 100 pM. Hence, this novel biosensor provided an opportunity for highly efficient N,S-NIR-CDs and EICA strategy in the analysis of biomarkers, which exhibited excellent potential in bioimaging and inchoate disease monitor.

N,S-NIR-CDs were produced according to the literature with slight modified [16]. Above all, 20 mL of formamide were mixed with 0.60 g of glutathione (GSH) and 0.03 g of folic acid with ultrasonic concussion for 20 min to make the solid dissolve completely. Then, above mixture was transferred from a beaker to a Teflon reactor, heating in the blast drying oven at 160 °C for 480 min. The dark liquid was transferred to a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate the large particle precipitation after cooling to below 30 °C. The supernatant was then put into a 3500 Da dialysis bag. After 2 d, the dialysate was filtered through a syringe filter with 0.22 μm membrane to remove the floccule. Finally, the atrovirens N,S-NIR-CDs product was obtained by vacuum-dried. Besides, nitrogen and sulfur codoped blue emission CDs (N,S-B-CDs) were prepared with 0.82 g citric acid (CA) and 0.30 g thiourea as precursors instead of 0.60 g GSH. The subsequent reaction and purification processes of N,S-B-CDs were the same as that of N,S-NIR-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: T7 Exo induced cascade amplification strategy.
ce:section.ce:para: First, H1, H2 and H3 (5 μM) were annealed to form the hairpin structure. Second, 10 μL of H1, 10 μL of H2, 10 μL of H3, 1 μL of 10 U/μL T7 Exo, 14 μL of TE buffer and 5 μL of different concentration of target miRNA-222 were mixed in a 200 μL centrifuge tube. Last, above-mentioned solution were cultivated at 37 °C for 120 min to obtain the output DNA (P2 and P3) and then inactivated the T7 Exo at 75 °C for 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ECL biosensor and its measurement procedure.
ce:section.ce:para: In the first place, all the bare GCE were cleaned by ultrasonic irrigation and polishing. Then, 10 μL of Au NPs were dropped on the GCE and dried at 37 °C. Afterward, 10 μL of 1 μM CP DNA was modified on the Au NPs/GCE overnight at 4 °C. Then, 10 μL of 1 mM hexanethiol (HT) was applied to the electrode at room temperature for 30 min to cover the remaining nonspecific sites. To get a signal off state, 10 μL of 1 μM QP DNA was incubated on above electrode at 37 °C for 120 min. After that, 10 μL of output DNA was modified on the GCE at 37 °C for 120 min to recover the ECL signal. Finally, above modified electrodes were tested in 50 mM S2O8 2− and 100 μg/mL N,S-NIR-CDs, which were dissolved with 2 mL of PBS. The necessary parameters of measurement are as follows: Excitation potential is − 1.0 to 0 V, photomultiplier tube (PMT) is 800 V and scan rate is 0.2 V/s.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), x-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectra were employed to investigate the morphologies and structure of N,S-NIR-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A, N,S-NIR-CDs were dispersed uniformly with a 2.26 nm diameter on average. Fig. 1B demonstrated that the height of N,S-NIR-CDs was 2.4 nm, indicating the quasi-spherical shape of CDs. As displayed in FT-IR graph (Fig. 1C), the peaks at 3370, 1682, 1605 and 1386 cm−1 were credited with the stretching vibration of OH, CO, CN/CC and CN, which consistent with the previous research [17]. In addition, XPS spectrum indicated the element types of N,S-NIR-CDs. According to Fig. 1D, four peaks at 162.2, 284.8, 399.6 and 531.2 eV were attributed to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS surveys were exhibited in Fig. S1, indicating the successful synthesis of N,S-NIR-CDs. Based on the Fig. 2E, XRD pattern demonstrated a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 24°, which conformed to the characteristic of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1F, Raman spectra of N,S-NIR-CDs displayed that the intensity of D band and the G band was 1588.14 a.u. and 3268.96 a.u. under the excitation of 532 nm. The intensity ratio of G and D (I G/I D) was 2.06, indicating the large C (sp2) conjugated domains of N,S-NIR-CDs [18].

First, the N,S-NIR-CDs' UV–vis absorption spectra exhibited two absorption bands at 360–450 nm and 600–700 nm (Fig. 2 A), which were probably produced by the aromatic structure's π–π* and n–π* transitions including CX (XO, N, S) [19]. Second, Fig. 2B revealed the fluorescence (FL) spectra of N,S-NIR-CDs. The optimum excitation peak was 425 nm and the maximum emission wavelength centered at 650 nm. The dilute solution of N,S-NIR-CDs displayed green color and emitted red light under a 420 nm flashlight (Fig. S2). Last, ECL emission spectrum of N,S-NIR-CDs was detected in S2O8 2− with a potential range from − 1.0 to 0 V. According to previous literature, ECL spectrum relied on the surface states while PL spectrum depended on the core states [20]. As shown in Fig. 2C, the ECL emission wavelength of N,S-NIR-CDs located at 680 nm and exhibited 30 nm redshift as compared with that of PL, demonstrating that there was surface trap state in N,S-NIR-CDs. In addition, the ECL emission peak of S2O8 2− solution was 615 nm under the same potential range (Fig. 2D), which was different from the N,S-NIR-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible ECL mechanism of N,S-NIR-CDs/S2O8 2− ECL system.
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated in curves b and c of Fig. 3 A, both N,S-NIR-CDs in PBS and bare GCE in S2O8 2− possessed almost negligible ECL signal. Significantly, the ECL intensity of N,S-NIR-CDs in S2O8 2− increased observably (curve a of Fig. 3A), indicating that the S2O8 2− as coreactant efficiently advanced the ECL emission of N,S-NIR-CDs. In addition, to further clarify the ECL mechanism of N,S-NIR-CDs/S2O8 2−, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was applied. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, N,S-NIR-CDs were tested in PBS (curve a) and deoxygenated PBS (curve b). The reduction peak of dissolved O2 at − 0.38 V significantly decreased after being treated with nitrogen. Nevertheless, the reduction peak of − 0.75 V unchanged, indicating that the N,S-NIR-CDs were reduced at − 0.75 V. Additionally, the N,S-NIR-CDs were examined in S2O8 2− as well. Compared with N,S-NIR-CDs in PBS (curve a, Fig. 3C), the peak current of N,S-NIR-CDs in S2O8 2− was noticeably higher (curve b, Fig. 3C), which further verify the coreaction effect of S2O8 2−. To sum up, the possible ECL mechanism of N,S-NIR-CDs/S2O8 2− were summed up in Supporting Information (2.4). In addition, a comparison table of different CDs as ECL emitter were listed in the Table 1 with important parameters including potential range and ECL emission wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the N,S-NIR-CDs and N,S-B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. 4 A, N,S-B-CDs were synthesized by changing the GSH into CA and thiourea. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the FL emission wavelength of N,S-B-CDs and N,S-NIR-CDs was 445 nm and 650 nm, respectively. In addition, the ECL emission peak of N,S-B-CDs and N,S-NIR-CDs was 550 nm and 680 nm, which has a certain of redshift compared to that of FL spectrum for both (Fig. 4C). Afterwards, DPV was applied to detect the possible reduction potential of N,S-B-CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 4D, the reduction peak of N,S-B-CDs at − 0.74 V did not change after being treated with nitrogen, indicating that the N,S-B-CDs were reduced at − 0.74 V, which was similar to that of N,S-NIR-CDs (Fig. 3B). The above results demonstrated that N and S doping could narrow the band gap to decrease the excitation potential [24], while the calculation of band energy was shown in Supporting Information 2.5. Subsequently, the Raman spectra of N,S-B-CDs was shown in Fig. 4E. The intensity of D band and the G band was 1362.90 a.u. and 1414.26 a.u., while intensity ratio of G and D (I G/I D) was 1.04, indicating the C (sp2) conjugated domains of N,S-B-CDs is small than that of N,S-NIR-CDs (Fig. 1F). Therefore, the introduction of precursors with long carbon chain effectively enlarged the C(sp2) conjugated domains of CDs to redshift the FL/ECL wavelength of N,S-NIR-CDs [25].

PAGE analysis was adopted to validate the successful design of EICA strategy and construction of ECL biosensor. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, samples in lanes 1–4 were miRNA-222 (T), H1, H2 and H3. Meanwhile, lane 5 was loaded with the hybridization product of H1 and T, which exhibited a higher migration band by contrast with that of lane 2, indicating the successful hybridization of H1 and T. Similarly, two obvious bands were shown in the top of lanes 6 and 8, demonstrating the effective formation of H1-T-H2 and H3-P2. In contrast, by mixing the samples of H1, T, H2 and T7 Exo, two obvious bands of H1-T and P2 were observed in lane 7, and the band of H1-T-H2 was disappeared, indicating the successful design of Cycle 1. Compared with lane 8, the band of H3-P2 was disappeared while a new band (P3) was observed in lane 9 after the addition of T7 Exo, which further indicated the successful operation of Cycle 2. Therefore, we speculated that EICA strategy was occurred as expected. In addition, the construction process of the ECL biosensor were shown in Fig. 5B. Lanes 1 and 2 were single stranded DNA (ssDNA) CP and QP. Then, lane 3 depicted that CP were successful hybridized with QP. Furthermore, when P2 and P3 were added in double stranded DNA CP-QP, two band in the top of lanes 4 and 5 were observed corresponding to CP-P2 and CP-P3, while the QP were released. Therefore, all the results demonstrated the correct design of DNA sequence.

First, as shown in Fig. S5, CV and ECL were applied to illustrate the successful construction of biosensor. Second, the concentration of T7 Exo and reaction time of EICA process were optimized in Fig. S6. After that, analysis of different concentration of miRNA-222 based on proposed ECL biosensor were carried out under the optimal conditions. The ECL intensity was continually raised as the incremental target concentration from 10 aM to 100 pM, as seen in Fig. 6 A. Meanwhile, a linear connection was observed between I ECL and the logarithm of miRNA-222 concentration, as shown by the calibration curve in Fig. 6B. The linear regression equation was I ECL = 24593.97 + 1069.97 lg c (R = 0.996), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 5.65 aM. This ECL biosensor possessed an exceptional linear range and a lower LOD when compared to conventional analytical techniques (Table 2 ).

The specificity and stability were two important indicators of a biosensor. As exhibited in Fig. 7 A, some other miRNAs (miRNA-21, miRNA-141 and miRNA-221) and their mixture were chosen as interferents with the concentration of 100 pM, which was 100 times greater than that of miRNA-222. The ECL intensity of other miRNAs were as low as that of the blank group. Besides, the ECL signals of the mixture group and miRNA-222 were partial unanimity. Above results demonstrated the good selectivity of constructed biosensor. Furthermore, Fig. 7B displayed the ECL response under 100 s continuous scanning (10 cycles), and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 1.51 % (1 pM) and 1.30 % (1 fM), which illustrated the excellent stability of proposed biosensor.

To assess the application ability of the ECL biosensor in actual samples, the lysates from HCCLM3 and MHCC-97L cells were utilized to detect the concentration of miRNA-222 [31]. As shown in Fig. 8 , the ECL signal were increased with the multiplication of cancer cells number from 101 to 105. In addition, the MHCC-97L cells displayed stronger ECL intensity than that of HCCLM3 cells, indicating that the miRNA-222 was high expression in MHCC-97L cells while low expression in HCCLM3 cells. And the above results were conformed to the gold standard method qRT-PCR (Fig. S8). Therefore, the constructed biosensor possessed certain application value in the early disease diagnosis.

In summary, a kind of novel CDs with NIR ECL emission and low excitation potential were synthesized as ECL emitters, which equipped with many advantages like good biocompatibility, low photochemical damage, deeper tissue penetration and less side reaction. Meanwhile, an improved EICA strategy was proposed by producing output DNA in dual-cycle, which tremendously enhanced the amplification efficiency of target miRNA-222. With the assist of N,S-NIR-CDs and EICA strategy, the constructed ECL biosensor realized the sensitive miRNA-222 analysis from 10 aM to 100 pM and successfully applied in the detection of cancer cell lysate. Therefore, this strategy constructed a neoteric NIR ECL emission CDs as emitter, which propelled the exploration of long wavelength CDs in ECL territory.

Yu-Zhuo Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rui Liu: Resources, Data curation. Ying Zeng: Investigation, Data curation. Ye-Yu Liao: Investigation. Jia-Li Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Ya-Qin Chai: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Ruo Yuan: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176153, 22374122, 22174113 and 22304141), Chongqing Postdoctoral Innovative Talent Support Program (7820100788) and Chongqing Postdoctoral Natural Science Foundation (CSTB2023NSCQ-BHX0009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.151057.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

The Experimental Details for reagents and materials, apparatus and corresponding Results and Discussion for high resolution XPS spectra of N,S-NIR-CDs, fluorescence image of N,S-NIR-CDs, optimized ECL excitation potential of N,S-NIR-CDs, possible ECL mechanism of N,S-NIR-CDs/S2O8 2-, calculation of band energy, construction and feasibility of the proposed ECL biosensor, optimal conditions of the ECL biosensor, LOD calculation, quantitative real-time PCR of miRNA-222, ECL efficiency of N,S-NIR-CDs and other different CDs.
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A tandem DNA nanomachines-supported electrochemiluminescence assay for attomolar detection of miRNA at ambient-temperature
Chem. Eng. J.
